What Is Claude Code Auto Memory? A Complete Guide to the Auto Memory Feature

If you've already been using Claude Code for development, you've probably hit this situation: every time you open a new conversation, Claude has total amnesia and you have to re-explain all the preferences you already told it about.
For me personally, I used to constantly tell Claude not to throw too many emojis into articles, but the next conversation it would start adding them again. Pretty annoying.
I previously wrote a post on

covering how to manually configure project rules with CLAUDE.md, but Claude Code's memory system isn't just CLAUDE.md. There's also a feature called Auto Memory that automatically jots down your corrections and preferences during conversation, so you don't have to repeat yourself next time.
What's the difference between Auto Memory and CLAUDE.md?
Quick rundown of the difference between these two before we dive in, because a lot of people get confused about what each one is for.

In short, CLAUDE.md is rules you actively write for Claude — things like "this project uses 2-space indent", "commit messages in English", and so on. Auto Memory is what Claude picks up from interacting with you. You don't have to write it — it remembers automatically.
If you want to learn more about CLAUDE.md, check out my earlier post
CLAUDE.md and Rules for AI writing tips (article in Mandarin)

Auto Memory is basically letting Claude take its own notes. When you correct Claude's behavior in a conversation, or give feedback about whether something worked well, Claude evaluates whether that information will be useful in future conversations. If it thinks it's worth keeping, it saves it automatically.
For example, say you tell Claude in a conversation:
Don't use too many emojis in articles, it looks too AI
Claude saves that feedback into a memory file. From then on, every new conversation loads that memory, so it remembers you don't like emojis.

But it doesn't write to memory in every conversation — Claude judges whether the info is worth keeping. If it's just a one-off discussion or something irrelevant to future conversations, it won't bother recording it.
Auto Memory requires Claude Code v2.1.59 or higher. Check with

Auto Memory files are stored in your Home directory under
A concrete example makes this clearer. If your project is at

/Users/ray/my-project/.claude/          ← Project config, in your project folder
/Users/ray/.claude/projects/-Users-ray-my-project/memory/  ← Auto Memory, in your Home directory
in them, but one is in your project folder and the other is in your Home directory — don't mix them up. So if you go digging in your project's
, that's normal. Memory files don't live there.
The memory path is derived from the Git repo, so subdirectories and Worktrees of the same repo share the same memory. If you're not in a Git repo, it falls back to the project root path.
is machine-local — it doesn't sync to other machines or cloud environments.
If you want to store memory somewhere else, set
This setting only accepts Policy, Local, and User levels — not Project-level
. The point is to prevent malicious settings in shared projects from redirecting memory somewhere it shouldn't go.
The memory directory mainly has two kinds of things:

~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/
├── MEMORY.md          # Index file, loaded every conversation
├── debugging.md       # Debug-related notes
├── api-conventions.md # API design decisions
└── ...                # Other topic files Claude creates on its own
is the index for the whole memory directory. Claude uses it to track what memories exist and which file each one lives in. Taking my own blog project as an example, my current

# Memory Index

- [feedback_writing_style.md](./feedback_writing_style.md) - Don't overuse 「」quotes and emojis in articles, it looks too AI
) - Don't overuse 「」quotes and emojis in articles, it looks too AI
contains the actual memory content, recording the fact that I don't like overusing quotes and emojis in articles.
Here's an important detail: only the first 200 lines or first 25KB (whichever is smaller) of
gets loaded at the start of each conversation. Anything past that doesn't get read. So Claude tries to keep
lean, splitting more detailed content into separate Topic files.
) aren't all loaded at startup — Claude reads them when it needs them. So even as your memory grows, it doesn't dump the entire memory directory into Context every time.
. CLAUDE.md, no matter how long, is fully loaded (though shorter usually means better adherence).
How can I tell when Claude is writing memory?
When you see "Writing memory" or "Recalled memory" in the Claude Code UI, that means Claude is updating or reading the memory files. You don't need to do anything — it handles it on its own.
How do I manage Auto Memory?

is the main entry point for managing memory. You can use it to see what memory and instruction files are loaded in the current conversation, toggle Auto Memory on/off, quickly open the memory folder, or pick any file to open in your editor.
Actively tell Claude to remember or forget
If you want Claude to remember something, just tell it directly in the conversation — for example, "remember: always use pnpm, not npm". Claude saves that to Auto Memory.
The reverse works too. If a memory is outdated, you can tell Claude "forget the previous rule about pnpm", and it'll update or delete the corresponding memory file.
Auto Memory files are just regular Markdown — you can go to the corresponding memory directory under
anytime and add, modify, or delete them by hand.
If you don't want Claude recording memory automatically, there are three ways to turn it off:
in a conversation and use the toggle inside
How do I see what Auto Memory has saved?
and pick the memory folder option to browse — they're all Markdown files, just open them up.
When should I use Auto Memory vs CLAUDE.md?

Quick guideline: stable, definite rules (code style, naming conventions, build process) belong in CLAUDE.md so the whole team shares them. Personal stuff, or stuff Claude picks up from interacting with you (your preferences, the gotchas you've hit, the way you debug a particular bug) — let Auto Memory handle that automatically.
Nope. Auto Memory files are stored on disk — they have nothing to do with Context compression.
only compresses the conversation log. After Compact, both
and CLAUDE.md get re-read from file and re-injected back into Context.

Tired of having to re-explain your preferences every time you open a new Claude Code conversation? Claude Code actually has an Auto Memory feature that automatically jots down your corrections and preferences during conversations, so you don't have to repeat yourself next time. This post walks through how Auto Memory works, where it's stored, how it gets loaded, and how to manage it.
